coredata.dc:title: Stable luminescent markers from sugar for patterning and pH sensing applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Ansi, V.A.
Renuka, N.K.
coredata.dc:description: In this article, the luminescence feature of sugar derived carbon dots is exploited for the fabrication of luminescent marker and pH sensor. A new environment benign material, Gum Arabic (GA) is introduced as a potential binder for luminescent markers for the first time. The marker was found to be highly photo stable and exhibited considerable adherence over a wide range of substrates. The luminescence intensity of the system is sequentially decreased with increment in pH of the medium, which enabled pH sensing using the system in the wide range of –10. 2-10.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Gum Arabic
Luminescent markers
pH sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of carbon dot-Gum Arabic mixtures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM (a), AFM (b) size distribution profile along the line in b (c), EDAX spectrum (d) [1(b), 1(c) and 1(d)- Reprinted from Sensors and actuators B: Chemical, 264, V.A Ansi, N.K Renuka, Table sugar derived Carbon dot – a naked eye sensor for toxic Pb2+ ions, 67–75, 2018, with permission from Elsevier], XRD pattern (e), FTIR spectrum (f) UV absorbance profile (g),Raman spectrum (h), Fluorescence spectrum (excitation wavelength 350 nm) along with the digital image of Cyan luminescence (i) and Photo-stability of the carbon dot solution towards UV irradiation for 2 (j).

Photo luminescent markers made using C-Dot-Gum Arabic combination. PL-Ink(left) and PL-Gel (right) under UV illumination showing the Cyan luminescence (a); digital images showing the pen filled with PL-ink (b) ; letters (c) and symbol (d) on non-luminescent paper under UV irradiation(Exc.365 nm).
Digital images of PL-gel coated substrates under UV light. (a) Cotton thread; (b) Glass plate; (c) Plastic sheet and (d) Paper imprinted with Hologram sticker.
Fluorescence response of the system with variation in pH of the medium (a); linear variation of fluorescence intensity with pH over a range of 2–10 (b).
Comparison of reported carbon dot for Fluorimetric determination of pH.

row.entry: Carbon dot covalently linked with fluorescein isothiocyanate

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: pH* pH of the sample measured using Mettler Toledo FE20 Five Easy Bench top pH Meter.
pH** pH measured using Carbon dot-Gum Arabic system.

head.ce:title: Stable luminescent markers from sugar for patterning and pH sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, 673 635, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, the luminescence feature of sugar derived carbon dots is exploited for the fabrication of luminescent marker and pH sensor. A new environment benign material, Gum Arabic (GA) is introduced as a potential binder for luminescent markers for the first time. The marker was found to be highly photo stable and exhibited considerable adherence over a wide range of substrates. The luminescence intensity of the system is sequentially decreased with increment in pH of the medium, which enabled pH sensing using the system in the wide range of –10. 2-10.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent materials including organic dyes, semiconductor based quantum dots, lanthanide compounds, metal–organic frameworks and carbon based nano dots have drawn immense recognition in all walks of life owing to their fascinating optical features [1–8], which attribute several applications to these materials [9–17]. Mimicking of valuable documents, currencies and products of branded companies is a growing global threat now a days, that challenges the well-being of the government as well as that of the public [18,16], where luminescent materials find role as security tags/markers to prevent this [19–27]. Major challenges that exist in this area are the expense associated with the synthesis strategy, higher solvent volatility, lack of stability /stickiness of the fabricated ink, decreased viscosity of the solvent medium, and poor stability towards external stimuli including acid, alkali and oil. Besides, the adhesive power towards various surfaces is also to be counted, as it is essential that the synthesised ink should be printable on different types of substrates. This article introduces an entirely new synthesis pathway to an extremely cheap, facile and environment friendly carbon dot based luminescent marker. Carbon nano dots beat all other members of the luminescent family for their credible features including low-toxicity, higher optical absorbance, excellent bio-compatibility, commendable chemical stability and extra-ordinary resistance to photo-bleaching [28–31]. Majority of the synthesis procedures for fine tuning the features and dimensions of carbon dots demand some sort of post-chemical treatments and surface passivation techniques that proceed through time consuming stringent experimental conditions [32–36]. Herein, an extremely facile, green and economic approach is adopted to yield bright luminescent carbon dots from sugar which has admirable photo physical features, like high quantum yield and commendable photo-stability. The system finds role as a successful pH sensor also over a wide range of pH values.

Though ink formulas may vary, general components of ink include a colorant (pigment/dye) and vehicle (binder). The medium of dispersion (binder) should be viscous enough to impart sticky nature to the luminescent ink for facilitating increased adherence over a range of surfaces. Previous works to this effect report the frequent usage of materials like Xylene, Methyl benzoate and Toluene. In addition to this, polymeric materials including Poly Vinyl Alcohol (PVA) and Poly Vinyl Chloride (PVC) gold medium and aqueous solution of Sodium hexa meta phosphate (SHMP) also served as dispersion medium for luminescent markers [37–39]. Herein, sucrose derived luminescent carbon dots perform as colorant that is dispersed homogeneously in Gum Arabic without forming any cluster or sedimentation. Gum Acacia (Gum Arabic -GA), harvested from the exterior of acacia tree has been proven to be a safe natural gum. Exploitation of the fine features of GA as a binder for possible application in luminescent markers has never been made so far. It is to be noticed that by fine tuning the concentrations of added GA medium it is possible to achieve luminescent coatings with two different consistencies; photo-luminescent ink (PL- ink) and photo-luminescent gel (PL- gel). PL- gel is meant for surface coating applications, whereas PL ink is useful for patterning. PL gel exhibits adherence over a wide range of surfaces including plastics, glass and fabrics.

Apart from the patterning applications, the system is also found to serve as an efficient tool for monitoring the pH of the medium via variation in luminescence intensity. Previous reports in this direction point out to the inability of the pH sensors to serve as a sensing tool covering extremely acidic and basic conditions. Most of them lack linear response with pH as they met cyclic pattern of sensing while moving from acidic to alkaline range. The present system is capable enough to monitor the pH over a wide range of pH values, vis., 2 to 10. Literature cites very few numbers of luminescence based pH sensors with appreciable activity under biologically relevant pH range. The proposed system is able to sense the aforesaid pH range with significant efficiency.

Sucrose (Merck) was subjected to caramelization adopting Maillard reaction [40], without any expensive and tedious post-chemical treatments, following the reported procedure [41]. Sucrose was heated at 180 °C for 3 min, which resulted in the formation of pale yellow slurry enriched with water molecules. Dilute ammonia (5 mL in 20 mL distilled water) (Merck) solution was added to the yellow slurry obtained and the mixture was subjected to continuous ultra-sonication for 12 h.

The synthesised carbon dot was adequately characterized by various techniques including High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy(HR-TEM), Atomic Force Microscopic imaging (AFM), Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), Fourier Transform Infra-Red spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy, Fluorescence spectroscopy and photo-stability measurements. Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope was employed for HR-TEM image recording. Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX) was conducted using FEI ESEM QUANTA 200 3D instrument. Surface topography of the sample was studied using Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, NTEGRE (NT-MDT)) equipped with NOVA software in semi-contact mode by recording two-dimensional images. XRD analysis was carried out using Rigaku Mini Flex-II diffractometer using Cu Kα1 radiation source. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) spectra were obtained using JASCO FTIR-4100 instrument using KBr pellet method. LZC-4X photoreactor was used to study the ultra violet radiation (UV) assisted reactions. UV–vis absorbance was monitored with the aid of JASCO V-550 spectrophotometer. The Fluorescence spectrum of carbon dot was obtained using Perkin Elmer Fluorescence spectrometer LS 55.

To monitor the variation in luminescence of the dots with pH of the medium, the prepared photo-luminescent system was treated with required amounts of phosphate buffer so as to tune the pH of the gel between acidic and alkaline values, proceeding through pH values. pH of the solutions was measured with the aid of Mettler Toledo FE20 Five Easy Benchtop pH meter.

Formation of spherical carbon nano particles with diameter around 3 nm is evident from the TEM image (Fig. 1 a), which is further backed by the AFM data (Fig. 1b and c). Inset of HRTEM image (Fig. 1a) shows the lattice fringe pattern with an in plane lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which signifies the (101) plane of graphitic systems, in agreement with previous reports [42]. The EDAX pattern confirms the presence of elements carbon and oxygen with atomic percentages of 73.04 and 26.96% respectively (Fig. 1d). Highly disordered carbon frame work inherent of carbon nano dots is evident in the XRD pattern, which showed distinct bands denoting (002) and (101) planes with interlayer spacing of 0.37 and 0.20 nm respectively (Fig. 1e). The inter layer spacing corresponding to diffraction from 002 plane of carbon dot is found to be higher than that of pristine graphite (0.34), due to the amorphous nature/oxygen functionalities [41]. The functional groups present in the system have been identified using FTIR spectral data (Fig. 1f). The band at 1000 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of COC epoxy functional group. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate groups give rise to bands at 1648 cm−1 & 1423 cm−1 respectively. The band at 3400 cm−1 can be attributed to OH stretching vibrations of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, and the shoulder band around 1700 cm−1 can be ascribed to asymmetric carboxylate stretching, which also accounts for the moisture content that may be present in the system. Thus FTIR spectrum shows that the core of carbon dots is well decorated with carboxylate, hydroxyl and epoxy functionalities. Fig. 1 (g) shows the broad range of UV absorbance around 210–280 nm. Raman spectral profile of the prepared carbon dot system also illustrates the presence of a highly functionalized nano carbon framework of graphitic nature (Fig. 1h), as evident from the D band (1298 cm−1) corresponding to sp3 hybridized carbon moieties. G band observed at 1597 cm−1 account for sp2 carbons in the system. The ID/ IG ratio provides a clear cut idea about the nature of carbon frame work inherent of the analyte. The ID/IG ratio of the system is found to be 1.2, indicating carbon core with decorated functionalities. The inherent luminescent nature of the carbon dots is depicted in Fig. 1(i), under excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The corresponding digital image of bright Cyan coloured fluorescent carbon dot solution is shown in Inset. It showed excitation dependent luminescence, a feature characteristic of carbon dots. Fig. 1(j) showcases the admirable resistance of the system towards prolonged irradiation with UV light, indicating its commendable photo-stability, which tunes the system as an effective PL marker/tag/ink with fine features.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of carbon dot-Gum Arabic mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dot solution with the aforesaid optical features is mixed with the naturally available Gum Arabic (GA) powder for the development of photo-luminescent tag/marker/ink. Consistency of carbon dot-GA mixture was tuned by altering the quantity of GA addition. It is quite noteworthy to mention that we could succeed in fabricating two PL markers with entirely different textures. Carbon dot-GA hybrid (1 ml & 10 mg respectively) turned out to be luminescent PL ink with some sort of semi-fluid nature compatible with the fluidity needed for commercially available inks, whereas the combination with 20 mg loading of GA to the same quantity of carbon dot solution resulted in the formation of fluorescent PL gel which can find promising applications in coating over surfaces of different textures. The UV and Fluorescence spectra of the gel used for the experiments (20 mg of Gum Arabic in 1 mL of carbon dot solution) are provided in Figs. S1 and S2 (Supplementary data). Only very little change is observed in the optical spectra of the gel when compared to pure carbon dots, which may be due to the higher percentage of binder in the gel. Both the PL-Ink and PL-Gel fluoresce with Cyan colour when kept under an ordinary UV lamp, as illustrated by the digital images displayed in Fig. 2 (a). For checking the practical applicability of the system, the PL-ink is filled inside an empty cartilage of gel pen and applied for writing purposes on a white paper, upon which the carbon dots adhere. The writing paper was selected in such a manner that no background UV fluorescence was observed inherently. Digital images showing the pen filled with PL-ink under UV light is also displayed (Fig. 2b). Letters and symbols made using the PL-ink showing strong fluorescence are also provided (Fig. 2c & d), indicating the bright future of the system in luminescent patterning.

The successful practical applicability of the prepared fluorescent PL-gel is demonstrated by coating the system over different surfaces. PL-gel showed strong adherence over different surfaces including natural fibre (cotton), glass plate, plastic sheet and paper. The above mentioned observation is found to be a significant achievement, foreseeing enormous amount of practical applications in daily life. Digital photographs showing the fluorescence of the PL-gel coated materials are displayed in Fig. 3 .

The synthesis was conducted without the addition of ammonia solution in order to check whether ammonia is having any vital role in deciding the fluorescence of obtained carbon dot units. It was observed that the carbon dots achieved via this method exhibit very feeble luminescence only. The fluorescence intensity was reduced to 1/3rd when compared to the particles obtained via ammonia assisted route. The presence of ammonia is expected to impart more oxygen containing functionalities on the surface, which is believed to be the route cause for the observed fluorescence.

As already mentioned, the luminescence intensity of the system is observed to be prone to pH change. The fluorescence response of the system is found to vary linearly over a wide range of pH (2–10). As the pH of the medium approaches alkaline nature, there occurs considerable amount of reduction in its luminescence intensity. Sequential quenching of fluorescence intensity with increment in pH of the medium (Fig. 4 a) and the linear variation between pH and fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4b) are evident, which ensure the utility of the system as a ratiometric pH sensor. There exist numerous reports on pH sensors with commendable efficiency. But it was observed that majority of the aforesaid sensors limit their sensing action to a very short span of pH. Comparison of pH sensing efficiency of the present system with the reported sensors is tabulated in Table 1 [43–51].

The major demerits that are attributed to the previously reported carbon dot based pH sensors are their adaptability to a narrow range of pH and the absence of sequential variation in their fluorescence response in accordance with the continuous variation of pH of the medium. Liu & Zhou have reported fluorescent carbon dots as efficient pH sensing tool which lacks ability for continuous monitoring of pH values [46]. The linear dependence of fluorescence intensity on the pH change is first observed in the range of pH 1.0–3.0, and after the interruption over a wide range including physiological pH, its sensing action is restored under alkaline pH values 9.0–12.5. Though the facileness and green nature of the adopted synthesis strategy can be claimed as a major highlight of the aforementioned sensor, its inactivity over a significant range of pH values decreases the credibility of this carbon dot as an efficient pH senor. Liu and co-workers have recently reported a carbon dot based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor for the detection of weak acids [47]. Shangguan and co-workers have developed a carbon dot- dye unit and employed for intracellular pH sensing in Hela cells working in the range of pH 5.2–8.8 [48]. Despite this broad range of pH adaptability, the incorporation of a toxic dye like Fuchsin which is mutagenic in nature can be counted as a major drawback of the aforesaid sensing tool. Though the FRET based pH sensor fabricated by Du and group is quite interesting [49], its sensing action is strictly limited to narrow range of pH, 4–7. N-doped carbon dot fabricated by Qian and co-workers is offers adaptability over a commendable range of pH (5–13.5) [50]. However, its indolence under acidic conditions fades its credibility. Recently, Liu and group were designed hydrophilic carbon dots (H-CDs) through anhydrous green method, which enable monitoring of a wide range of pH (3–13) in a linear fashion [51]. Though the above mentioned range is obviously attractive, the demand of higher synthesis temperature (250⁰C) and usage of organic solvents (petroleum ether) as the extracting medium decreases its wide applicability.

Viability of the proposed Carbondot-Gum Arabic unit as a pH sensor is monitored by using the system for testing the pH of some real samples. The real samples selected for probing the pH sensing efficacy of present system are frozen orange pulp, tomato pulp, curd, egg yolk and soap solution which were labelled as sample A,B,C,D and E respectively. pH of these five samples were measured using Mettler Toledo FE 20 Five Easy Bench top pH meter and was compared with the values obtained using the system (Table 2 ). All the measurements were carried out in triplicate and the corresponding standard deviation were also incorporated. The negligible error percentages associated with each measurement assure the reliability of the proposed fluorescent pH sensor.

Herein, sucrose derived carbon dots with adorable photo-physical features are made use of for the fabrication of luminescent marker and pH sensor. An environmentally benign material, Gum Arabic is introduced as a potent binder for the luminescent marker for the first time. Two different concentrations of this natural binder is mixed with fluorescent carbon dots, labelled as PL-Ink and PL-Gel and practical applicability of these entities as PL marker/security tag was examined by imprinting/coating them on commercially available non-fluorescent paper and other common substrates. Apart from this, fluorescence of the present system is found to decrease sequentially when pH of the system moves from acidic to basic range (2 to 10). This variation of luminescence intensity was found to be perfectly linear with the pH alteration. The practical applicability and the reliability of the proposed pH sensor were monitored by sensing the pH of a number of real samples with wide range of pH. The negligible error percentages associated with pH measurement of real samples is sufficient enough to highlight the utility of the system as a layman’s tool.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: V.A. Ansi gratefully acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala Kerala government and the facilities received from the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut. The authors thank CSIR-National Chemical Laboratory, Pune for providing the HRTEM and EDAX analysis. AFM analysis of the sample was carried out at CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2019.03.097.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
